title: Retrieval Is a Recall Step, Not a Precision Step
concept: Initial retrieval casts a wide, fast, imprecise net; reranking narrows it down with a slower, more accurate model.

exercises.prompt: What is the common LLM architecture pattern that connects external data search with a generative model?
exercises.explain: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) is the foundational pattern of combining search with LLMs, which sets the stage for optimizing it with reranking.

cards.title: The Needle in a Million Haystacks
cards.body: When searching through millions of documents, you cannot afford to have a smart LLM read every single one. You need a fast way to filter, followed by a smart way to choose.

cards.title: Recall vs. Precision
cards.body: Retrieval is designed for high recall, meaning it quickly grabs a broad pool of candidates. Reranking is designed for high precision, sorting those candidates using a smarter, slower model.

cards.title: The Fishing Net vs. The Marine Biologist
cards.body: Think of retrieval like a giant fishing net that scoops up hundreds of fish quickly, while reranking is a marine biologist who inspects each fish individually to select the perfect one.

cards.title: A Real-World Pipeline
cards.body: A vector search database scans millions of articles in milliseconds and returns the top 50 matches. Then, a Cohere or Cross-Encoder reranker analyzes those 50 matches and picks the single best answer.

cards.title: Vector Search Isn't Magic
cards.body: Many developers think vector search is perfect at finding the exact right document. In reality, vector search often returns irrelevant results that look similar on the surface, which is why a second check is needed.

cards.title: Retrieval vs. Reranking
cards.body: Compare how the two steps handle data to achieve both speed and accuracy.

left.text: Casts a wide net. Fast, high recall, but includes irrelevant clutter.

right.text: Inspects candidates closely. Slower, high precision, picks the best.

exercises.prompt: What is the primary goal of the initial retrieval step?

options.text: To perfectly rank the documents in order of relevance
To quickly gather a broad set of potentially relevant documents
To summarize the documents for the user
To translate the query into multiple languages

exercises.explain: Retrieval's main job is high recall, meaning it wants to gather all possible matches quickly without worrying yet about perfect order.

exercises.prompt: True or False: The initial retrieval step is generally faster but less precise than the reranking step.
exercises.explain: Retrieval is optimized for speed and recall, while reranking is optimized for accuracy and precision.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing words describing the nature of the initial retrieval step:
exercises.sentence: The initial retrieval step is fast and casts a wide, imprecise net.
exercises.explain: Initial retrieval needs to be extremely fast, so it casts a wide, imprecise net to gather many potential matches before they are refined.

exercises.prompt: Which of these explanations sounds like it was written by an AI trying to sound overly complex?

options.text: We use retrieval to get a quick list of possible matches, and reranking to sort them correctly.
Retrieval is about speed and finding everything that might be relevant; reranking is about accuracy.
The vector database leverages multi-dimensional distance metrics to maximize document candidate recall latency optimization.

exercises.explain: Option C is typical AI-generated fluff using overly complex jargon to describe a simple query pipeline. Options A and B are natural, straightforward explanations.

exercises.prompt: Why do we split LLM search systems into a fast retrieval step followed by a slower reranking step?

options.text: To quickly capture all potentially relevant items first, then carefully select the best ones
To make the system slower and add extra steps to the pipeline
Because vector databases are bad at handling text data
To replace the LLM entirely with a simple lookup table

exercises.explain: Retrieval prioritizes recall (finding all potentially relevant documents quickly), which naturally brings in noise. Reranking acts as a precision filter, sorting the top results with high accuracy. Together, they create a fast and highly accurate pipeline.
